author: @rizqbeckett7086
text: What’s amazing about this movie is the things that I notice almost every time I watch it. I noticed that Ilsa has never told her husband that she loves him, although he often declares his love to her and not only in words, but she does say it to Rick. But there’s no denying that she admires her husband and it’s apparent why, in spite of the threats from the Nazis his courage and commitment is outstanding. There’s an incident that causes Rick to switch his mind from his original plan to allow Victor to get away alone without his wife. I think it’s when Victor unselfishly asks Rick to use the letters of transits to take his wife away after telling Rick to his face that he knows that he loves his wife too. I also think that Rick hated when Louie compared his low immoral self to him. The key word was when he asked her to trust him. Because in the next scene when Louie tried to arrest Victor….well the rest of the movie is perhaps one of the greatest endings in movie history with four words before walking away with Louie, “Here’s looking at you kid!”.

author: @jackrice2770
text:
I see a few film students have been assigned this film. They should note a couple of important things: 

1) Note there isn't any 'filler', not one unnecessary scene. 

2) Instead of the modern film style with naked, sweaty bodies flopping around for several minutes, here we see symbols and subtle shots that tell us that yes, Ilsa and Rick "do it" but we're spared the pace-killing soft-core porn so prevalent today. (That shot of the searchlight when the camera cuts away). Yes, Bogie and Ilsa are wearing clothes in the next shot, but apparently folks in the '40s were a bit quicker on the uptake than today. 

3) The script is tight, and although at least three writers worked on it, the final result works perfectly. The sign of a director in complete control of his project.

4) The shadows and camera angles are works of genius. Lighting designers and cinematographers should watch it frame by frame.

author: @jackrice2770
text:
Yes, in case you're a young'un, this movie has been voted one of the best by any number of film associations, One historical note: Renault says to Strasser that "he was with the Americans when they blundered into Berlin in 1918." Germany was never invaded during the First World War, and certainly no foreign troops, American or otherwise, entered the capital. Of course, one shouldn't rely on films for historical facts (actually, almost all 'historical' films are grossly inaccurate).

Here's looking at you, Kid.

author: @timothydavis1885
text: There have been few movies that have equaled the romantic twists, the suspense, the drama, the musical soundtrack, as Casablanca.   As Time Goes By will go down in cinematic history as one of the greatest musical masterpieces of all time.   This was undoubtedly Humphrey Bogart's greatest work. Humphrey Bogart was unquestionably one of the greatest actors ever to grace movie screen.  Ingrid Bergman will forever be one of the most beautiful woman ever to cross Hollywood, whose theatrical performance in Casablanca seemed as though she were living out her own life, which just happened to be portrayed in this timeless work of cinematic art.   Paul Heinreid was the ultimate patriot, a part he played so well, it would go down as one of the most realistic performances ever.  This movie is without doubt one of the top 10 movies Hollywood ever produced.

author: @gemanscombe4985
text: Rick wasn't helping Victor and Ilsa until she explained her train station no-show. He finally got that, then had to get Victor out of Captain Reynaud's custody and set up the pair's clean getaway. Rick realized that him and Ilsa having the hots for each other paled beside the importance of Victor's mission, and that staying together in Casablanca was impossibly dangerous. The script writers made sure Rick didn't have any easy outs, just a tough one.

author: @gemanscombe4985
text: "Kid" was colloquial of the time, like "kiddo" and "Bud". Rick wasn't calling her a child or a young goat. Bogart was 42, playing 37. Bergman was 27, playing 20-something, not 15. From what I've read, "Here's looking at you, kid" was what Bogart said when playing poker and calling for the cards to be shown. He used it here as a toast or pet phrase of endearment.

I think it's simpler than that. Love dude. Her husband loves his political fight more than he loves her, he could have avoided being captured on several different occasions if he just stopped his rebelling, but that's what he put first. Also he could have listened to her and not gone to the secret meeting and avoided capture.


Rick LOVES HER more than anything and didn't think twice to give up anything to help her.

author: @rizqbeckett7086
text: @cameroncordova6448I have to disagree. The obvious thing is the age difference. Both men seem to be way older than her, when she explained to Rick how they found her and explained that Victor had escaped and was wounded, she sounded compromised because she was in fact a married woman, which clearly leaves the door open to speculate that her relationship with Rick was sexual. Now the nature of Victor’s injury was vague, but it was mentioned that he was tortured. So let’s just say for shits and giggles that the Nazis had tortured him that resulted in lifelong impotence, she wouldn’t have known that until she went back to him. And of course she had no way of knowing that when they arrived in Casablanca, and was informed that they were to meet someone who had the all important letters of transit at Rick’s Cafe Americana, that she would first see Sam and then Rick. I believe that prior to them running into each other she had accepted a sexless existence with a man that she loved. Until…

author: @tiffsaver
text:
@Rob-157 
For so many years I couldn't understand the attraction for Bogart, who was old, ugly, and short.  Not only that, but he had a dental problem that gave him a decidedly odd diction problem.  But after seeing him as 'Rick,' suddenly all of my petty criticisms disappeared.  I think Bogie was the single best example of "the tough guy with a heart of gold" that Hollywood ever produced.  Just watch his Academy Award performance in "The African Queen."  It was pure magic.

author: @tiffsaver
text: What actor today could have ever replaced these screen icons, from Humphry Bogart, to Ingrid Bergman, to Peter Lorre, to Sidney Greenstreet, to Claude Rains??  Absolutely NO ONE.

author: @anglophils645
text: This one should never be remade.  No one could come close to duplicating the performances of Bogart, Bergman, Henried, or Rains.  These actors each had such a unique style------I don't believe that any combination of actors today could come together the way that they did.  The re-make of "Sebrina" fell flat.  Even with stars like Harrison Ford, Richard Kinnear, and Julia Ormond, the 1995 version does not compare to the 1954 original, starring Humphrey Bogart, William Holden, and Audrey Hepburn.  I fear the same would happen with an attempt to re-make "Casablanca".

author: @anglophils645
text: @tiffsaverThe only classic that I can think of, where the re-make is possibly as good or better than the original, is Pride and Prejudice.  The original starring Greer Garson and Lawrence Olivier, is not necessarily better than the 1995 version starring Colin Firth and Jennifer Ehle.  The 2005 version starring Keira Knightley and Donald Sutherland, among others, is also very good.

author: @tiffsaver
text:
@anglophils645 
Same thing with sequels.  But for my money, the best, and very first sequel of all time is "The Bride of Frankenstein," in 1935.

author: @Just_another_Euro_dude
text:
Old classic movies are still fantastic as any from the current age. Maybe few tiny details change throughout the decades, maybe a bit of mentality changes, and a LOT of technology changes, but the ESSENCE of the quality REMAINS, regardless if it's 1920s, 30s, 40s ,50s, 60s ,70s, 80s, 90s, 00s, 10s or 20s (again 😅)...
Few years ago i was naive enough to believe that old black and white movies are somehow inferior to the more modern ones, let's say starting from the 1980s and forward. But then i started watching a lot of the classics from the golden age of Hollywood and from the European cinema and from the Japanese cinema and boy i was so wrong. Watched the Bicycle thieves, watched the Seven Samurai, watched the White Heat with James Cagney, and Public Enemy, and also Scarface with Paul Muni. The method of the acting might be somewhat strange in those early 1930s movies i admit, (the infamous overacting) Public Enemy and The Scarface, but boy the classics from the 1940s and 1950s are just perfect. What Kurosawa did with The Seven samurai in 1954 was just an incredible achievement,  (almost a PERFECTION of a movie) and the emotion and warmth of the Italian Bicycle thieves from the 1948 is just something i didn't find in ANY movie of those times. It's amazing really. Classic is a classic regardless of the year but you need a bit of the life experience to realise that. It was impossible for me to realise that in my 20s or even in most of my 30s. I was the modern movies elitist. And was so soooo wrong. 😂 While interestingly enough i loved watching the old movies as a kid. Now in my early 40s i can't get enough of the old classics while STILL loving the modern cinema.

text:
Fun fact...high quality black-and-white films from the 1930s had at least 10k resolution by modern digital standards.
Even after the original is scanned in 4K the result still would not be as "sharp" as on the actual movie screen 80+ years ago.
(Have a look at  your ancient family photos....if  the camera was focussed correctly every hair on your great- grandma's head was in  sharp focus.)

I totally understand the travel tiredness. Burnout happens to the best of us. author: @Boug0014
text: It’s quite interesting with those street guides.  Some (I’m not saying all), don’t even know the history …. The Portuguese never occupied Rabat…. They occupied Maamoura in 1515 about 30km north of Rabat and Casablanca in also 1515.  But they never were in Rabat.  The Oudaya Kasbah was built by the Almohad Royal Family in 1150 as a fortification (Ribat) to fight the Berghwata Kingdom that extended from south of Rabat to about Essaouira.  The Kasbah was later used as a settlement for the Andalus Refugees after the fall of Granada and the expulsion of the Moriscos from Spain.

author: @J_Ges
text:
Hi Chris, compliments on your videos, I really enjoy them!
I’m a street photographer living in London and in March I’m going for a week in Marrakech. I’ve heard that sometimes people can be a bit hostile if they notice they’re being photographed, or may ask for money. I always try to be very respectful, quiet and discreet when I shoot, but I was wondering what your honest opinion is. If I’m travelling alone for a week, do you think a city like Rabat or Fez might be a better option for street photography, with people being a bit less confrontational? I’d really appreciate your thoughts. Thanks again for the great content.

author: @georgimihov2690
text: Old graves by/on the beach is actually a very common thing around the world - sunbathing and spending time on the beach is only a thing of the modern era. In previous centuries people had no use for beaches, as they are not arable land, so instead of creating cemeteries on good fertile land inland they would put them along a beach, or on the beach in some instances, to make use of otherwise useless space.

author: @LRPhotographer
text: I've been to Morocco three times, and I've made it a point to go to the medina in Rabat every time, no matter where else in the country I traveled. But, the whole country leaves me homesick for it every time I return to the U.S. I hope to go back again someday soon and to finally be able to take my husband with me. The Rabat medina will definitely be on our itinerary. And, even being from the southern U.S., which is known for its hospitality, I can confidently (though somewhat sadly) say we've got nothing on the hospitality of Moroccans. Even visiting the very first time feels like going home. 🥰🥰🥰

author: @NajahAzzouzi
text: While I appreciate the attention you gave Morocco and Marrakech, I was quite annoyed by the way you handled and reported the snake charmers’ situation. Holding 20 dirhams (the equivalent of $2) in your hands, like some sort of protective strategy to get the snake charmers to let you film them alone was insulting. Then, you went on to describe the interaction like some sort of primitive encounter (that was not expected how it unfolded, you said). They took your camera to film the cobra up close, and you ended up with a great shot!! That didn’t seem to redeem them in your eyes. As to putting a snake around your neck, that’s standard practice, and, I believe, you must have researched it prior to going. I don’t think you said that out of malice, yet your “ classic Western tourist approach” is insulting and reinforces the civilizational/colonial prejudices.

author: @jgvarea
text:
Greetings. 
The sweets you show at 9:15 are mass-produced and don't represent the exquisite Moroccan pastry.
They contain more peanuts than almonds and sugary syrups instead of honey, and the flours are usually of poor quality.
To enjoy true Moroccan pastries, you have to go to Pastiseria Le Gato in Marrakech, among many others; they're nothing like what you find in the souks.

author: @ThomasRebelo-s3e4n
text:
Marrakesh is like the NEw York of Morocco .  The Medina is full of tourists milling around and the Medina is a constant hum of mopeds and scooters buzzing up the middle.  She said occasional i would say constant.  

  anywhere but Marrakesh would be my suggestion.  Fez or Chefchouan for an authentic experience.   Found a hamman in Marrakesh near the Grand old Mosque.  The old islamic college worth a visit.  The Bahia Palace under construction so sights limited.  I agree with photography paradise.  stay away from snake charmers..big scam.
but remember:  ripping off tourist , or worst hurting or assaulting a tourist is jail time, no questions ask.   So no matter what they demamd from u they wont do anything to you.  Military dictatorships are great for tourists. 

Negotiating is their national sport.  You will get ripped off (relatively speaking) they are just better that you.  you will end up buying a carpet u didnt think you needed.  the best way is to sample a price for let us say a leather bag. go to 3 different shops to get a sample price then you will have an idea what the price would be .  offer half and be ready to walk away .  u gotta be kind of rude . 
as for food , go outside Medina and you will find a grill smell.  look for a big pot on counter , it means soup is available for 10 MAD.  
it is a great country ,  people are generally nice ....explore the towns outside marrakesh

author: @KristinasTravels
text:
Apologies, this was not our intention. 
The sweets joke was just comic relief, as we don’t have open air sweet stores in Australia, so it’s just something that came to mind and we had a little chuckle about. Of course we didn’t think they were actually old, otherwise we wouldn’t have eaten them. 

In regards to the train station, we actually had a couple of uncomfortable encounters onboard the train. One man tried to befriend us and asked many personal questions, only to be escorted out of our carriage as he didn’t have a valid ticket to be in our booth. We also had another 3 people be rowdy, eat greasy food and drink while on the lookout for the guard, only to be also kicked out of our carriage which also made us feel uncomfortable. So, these situations, while they can happen everywhere, and in any country, did happen to us first hand. We chose not to include these stories in our report. 

Thank you for watching all the same, we had a fabulous time in Morocco, but we were also very much out of our comfort zone at times, but this is just one experience, and our experience. I’m sure everyone has a different story to tell 🙂

author: @paullefko
text:
Rent a car; hire a driver; go see the real Morroco and avoid the tourist stuff. A car is about 400 a week a driver is 150 a day. Not only are you wasting time sitting on train, you’re missing out on cities and towns along the journey. And by taking “tours” you’re locked into an agenda and if you want to explore more, well you can’t. 
Cash is king there; credit cards are rarely used around the country. 

Honnêtement, la vidéo est sympa, mais elle ne rend pas justice à l'authenticité du Maroc. Si vous y retournez, sortez de votre zone de confort touristique. J'apprécie que vous ayez opté pour des visites guidées par des locaux ; trouvez maintenant un guide local qui vous emmènera découvrir des endroits hors des sentiers battus.

Real tips: if you see it a line of tourists to go in… yeah pass; 2. Restaurants- the more the ranking and tourists… yeah no ;go to mom and pops. 
Coffee is awesome; but do an expresso with an Americano with milk. Big boost in taste. 
Tea is meant to sipped slowly
Don’t eat with your left hand, Moroccans don’t necessarily use utensils.

Demandez aux gens de  prendre en photo; beaucoup refusent. Utiliser de gros appareils photo et du matériel vidéo est très mal vu ; vous avez probablement attiré beaucoup de regards à cause de votre équipement.

author: @artdirector9517
text: Nice vlog 👌Yeah Kingdom of Morocco is stuning as package, his landscapes is incredibley diverse. Sahara desert, oasis, woods, snow, rivers, two water fronts long ocean coastline and mediterranean golden sand. surf spot, mountains, old medinas, vibrant souks, festivals, colorful cities etc.. He has too a rich culture of food, music, hand craft, traditionnel clothing, home furnitur, interior design, tiles etc.. As you can see in Marrakech city as berber saharien or in Tanger as mauresque with some spanish archetecture touch, the city that contains the world's first United States embassy in the history. Also kingdom of Morocco is the first country to recognize USA in the world at 1777. We are so open as civilisation for exemple about the spanish influences, they were in the north of Morocco for almost 50 yeras but not problem becaus we were in their country for 800 years 😊 that's pretty much isn't ?! lool. We left the last city Grenada at january of 1492, Just a few month later, they discovered America at october of 92 by Columbus using our tools. they translated too our books left in the university of Cordoba the best university in the world at that time. Two years after Spain become the new bigest super power on earth with Portugal. Why not ?! nothing was missing 🙂. Just for information it's the empire of Morocco who built almost 60 % of spanish cities. for exemple Garnata city become Grenada, Ichbillia city become Sevilla, kortoba become Cordoba, Balancia become Valencia etc.. It's so easy isn't ! 😎 as it is for the 40 other cities. All this legacy of sciences has crossed borders of the Andalousie reached France then to England and Florence city in Italy by Sicily island to declare the edge of renaissance in europe. For information, the oldest university in the world called Al-Qarawiyyin in Fez city and while there were 400 Hamam (bath) in Sevilla city and oil-powered lamp-posts in her streets there were any one in Paris or London. also is the oldest kingdom systeme in the world too with japan .Any way we are brothers every one of us carries the torch of progress. For reference, Steve Jobs' father is Syrian but the most signifiant of all this is the first traveler in the history and the first who draw a map of the planet earth were too moroccans, They are Ibn Battuta and Al-Idrissi. So strange isn't ?! 🤔 especialy it was a right map even without satellite 😅. Any way last time Morocco become the hidden paradise of surfers, motobikers, caravaners, stylists, artist, photographers, and digital nomades 🤫 in short when god reduces the world in half millions klm he gives Mor-occo. Some streets activities prices : Henee hand tatoe cost like 8 dollars. Tips for musicians like 2 dollars but if you film with them a dance, more. Tips for Sneaks charmer like 2 dollars but if you shoot a vlog video with them, more. Short Camel ride or pigmy horse ride from 5 dollars. the golden advice here is : fixed the price with each one of them before to make easier your travel. Just free you mind and joy it as you can. author: @Karikorak2005
text:
🔥🔥 Cameroon is the second-most successful team in the history of the Africa Cup of Nations (AFCON) with five titles, that councided with Issa Hayatou's leadership at the Confederation of African Football (CAF).His leadership saw significant growth in the tournament and African football's global standing, including periods of Cameroonian succes . The period between the 1980s and early 2000s marked a dominant era for Cameroon, during which they secured four of their five titles during his time as CAF president . All together cameroon won the title five times (1984, 1988, 2000, 2002, 2017), making them second only to Egypt in total wins, and have reached the finals seven times, appearing in several consecutive finals during the 80s and early 2000s, solidifying their legacy as "The Indomitable Lions". Meanwhile, The Cameroonians, Issa Hayatou's Leadership of CAF  was the fifth and longest-serving president of the Confederation of African Football (CAF), holding the position for 29 years from 1988 to 2017. His tenure was marked by both significant development and controversy, but he undeniably transformed African football: 
Expansion of Competitions: Hayatou oversaw the expansion of the AFCON final tournament from 8 teams to 16 teams, providing more opportunities for nations to compete. He also introduced youth and women's championships.
Increased World Cup Representation: He successfully advocated for an increase in Africa's guaranteed spots at the FIFA World Cup, rising from two teams in 1990 to five by 1998.
Global Recognition: During his presidency, African teams achieved historic results, including Cameroon reaching the World Cup quarter-finals in 1990 and winning the Olympic gold medal in 2000. He was also instrumental in bringing the World Cup to South Africa in 2010, the first time it was held on the African continent.
Financial Growth: CAF's finances significantly improved under his leadership, growing from $1.25 million to $130 million in the black by the time he left office. 
While Hayatou's leadership coincided with much of Cameroon's success, particularly in the 1990 and 2000s, his role as CAF president was as an administrator for the entire continent, focused on general growth and global standing, rather than directly managing the Cameroonian national team's performance.

text:
Khouya means my brother , Sahbi means my friend.

The word “habibi” is commonly used in the Middle East to mean “my dear” or “my love.” In Morocco, however, people often say “hbibi” (without the “a”) to address someone dear or close, such as a friend or a loved one, and it does not necessarily have a romantic meaning.

author: @SueHutton
text: I see this was made in 2010. Many big changes in Tangier road systems since then and there is a project to 'join up' Tangier and Malabata bays. The view will be amazing as cruise liners come into port. If anyone is up, that is. They tend to come in before dawn. The medina is just the same. Oh, and the clubs and bars along the corniche have all been demolished for a vision of a 'clean' Tangier which is family friendly. That area will also look on to a marina. The fishing port is being shunted sideways. They're supposed to be trying to keep the walls of the Kasbah but they look very precarious.

Let's make some things clear here:
1- Moorish architecture is THE moroccan architecture which is the local indigenous one.
2-Tangier takes its name from Tingis who was actually the daughter of Atlas king of Mauretania (ancient Morocco).
3-The blue of Chefchaouen is not recent as some houses were already painted blue decades before 1970 then it spread all over the town later on.

author: @fouedfoued5692
text:
_Tangier , indeed can boast of being one of the very few cities on earth where almost all the great civilizations have felt attracted and drawn to for they've all left their mark or legacy , from the Levant Phoenicians (more than a millennium b.c) , followed by the Greeks , then the Roman till the Syrio-Arab Umayyads...Tangier is one the few emplacements where Gods and winds collide , where Heracles defeated the God Antaeus (Greek mytho|ogy) , after that the Greek Colossus seized the Golden Apple in Lixus (Today's Larache).._
_Tangier the Silent witness of one of the biggest naval collisions between the British Nelson’s fleet (Which was frequently stationed in front of Cap Spartel) Vs the Napoleon's Franco-Spanish great Armada in the Trafalgar campaign in the Strait of G¡braltar l8o5.._
_Just a couple of stories (among others) from my beloved Tangier.._

author: @islamisthetruewaytogod6812
text:
Hello ! May Allah protect and guide you to his light and happiness in this life and the hereafter, God bless, Ameen. Excuse me for giving a little presentation of Islam, because it is very misunderstood nowadays, especially on those « Antichrist's » times, where media and politics are mixed to distort history and truth. And terrorists (puppets of the Antichrist) who misinterpret verses, out of ignorance and political motivations, and take them out of historical context (just like radical atheists do by the way) don't help either. Thank you very much for your time.

First of all, if you have any questions regarding Islam and yourself, just open the Quran randomly, and you will find the answer in front of you, like a miracle and a sign from God.
	
Islam is an arabic word that means the Surrender to the One and Only God, our Creator, Protector, Provider, who gives us life and all that we have, we are safe and sound by his will and grace, we are His and to Him we return, and we have to thank him in this trial life by submitting to him by our free will, or later in the Day of judgment when it's too late to save our own skin. Islam was the original Religion descended to earth from heaven with Adam and Eve (peace and blessing be upon them) in the beginning of humanity. and was passed to people with the succession of the 124 000 prophets and 315 messengers of God to all nations and civilizations since, passing by Noah, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Ismaël, Joseph, Moses, Aaron, Joshua, David, Solomon and Jesus (Peace and blessing be upon them) during the history of mankind, the last replaces and completes the previous, until the succession of the last messenger of God fourteen centuries ago, Muhammad (Peace and blessing be upon him) to complete the noble morals of all mankind, to bring humans and jinns out of darkness into light, and to purify people's religion and belief from corruption and polytheism, and return it to purity and true monotheism, like it was in the times of the prophets (Peace and blessing be upon them).

Many Religions that we know nowadays, at their beginning were true and under Islam, initiated by one of the prophets of God, but their original teachings, history and scriptures have been corrupted over time with falsification and polytheism, or lost and replaced with false ones. That's why Islam is the only Religion accepted by God nowadays, which consists in bearing witness that there is no god besides Allah (God in Aramaic, the original language of Jesus and the Gospel), and that Muhammad is His servant and messenger, just like Jesus and Moses and others are His servants and messengers. Never a messenger of God said he was God or literally son of God, it was the people after him who changed the words of God and corrupted the Religion. God is unique and absolute, He does not need to have a family and sons or to associate anyone else with His kingdom, He can simply create whatever He wants, everything belongs to Him, and to Him everything will return. Allah said in Surah Al-Mu’minun : “God has never begotten a son, nor is there any god besides Him. Otherwise, each god would have taken away what it has created, and some of them would have gained supremacy over others. Glory be to God, far beyond what they describe. The Knower of the hidden and the manifest. He is exalted, far above what they associate. (91-92 / Translated by ITANI).

Allah means the one and only God, the God of all prophets and creatures, the creator of the universe and mankind, and the Master of the Day of judgment, where our destiny, Hell or Paradise, is decided based on our faith and deeds in this trial life, and above all, Allah's mercy. 

Allah said in Surah Al-Ikhlas : In the name of God, the Gracious, the Merciful.
Say, “He is God, the One. God, the Absolute. He begets not, nor was He begotten. And there is none comparable to Him.” (1-4 / Translated by ITANI).

Allah said in Surah An-Nisa : O FOLLOWERS of the Gospel! Do not overstep the bounds [of truth] in your religious beliefs, and do not say of God anything but the truth. The Christ Jesus, son of Mary, was but God's Apostle - [the fulfilment of] His promise which He had conveyed unto Mary - and a soul created by Him. Believe, then, in God and His apostles, and do not say, "[God is] a trinity". Desist [from this assertion] for your own good. God is but One God; utterly remote is He, in His glory, from having a son: unto Him belongs all that is in the heavens and all that is on earth; and none is as worthy of trust as God. Never did the Christ feel too proud to be God's servant, nor do the angels who are near unto Him. And those who feel too proud to serve Him and glory in their arrogance [should know that on Judgment Day] He will gather them all unto Himself: (171-172 / Translated by Muhammad Asad).

Allah the Most Merciful said in Surah Ali-Imran : Behold, the only [true] religion in the sight of God is [man's] self-surrender unto Him; and those who were vouchsafed revelation aforetime took, out of mutual jealousy, to divergent views [on this point] only after knowledge [thereof] had come unto them. But as for him who denies the truth of God's messages - behold, God is swift in reckoning!
Thus, [O Prophet,] if they argue with thee, say, "I have surrendered my whole being unto God, and [so have] all who follow me!" - and ask those who have been vouchsafed revelation aforetime, as well as all unlettered people, "Have you [too] surrendered yourselves unto Him?" And if they surrender themselves unto Him, they are on the right path; but if they turn away - behold, thy duty is no more than to deliver the message: for God sees all that is in [the hearts of] His creatures.
Verily, as for those who deny the truth of God's messages, and slay the prophets against all right, and slay people who enjoin equity - announce unto them a grievous chastisement.
It is they whose works shall come to nought both in this world and in the life to come; and they shall have none to succour them.
(19-22 / Translated by Muhammad Asad)..

God said : Say, “We believe in Allah, and in what was revealed to us, and in what was revealed to Abraham, and Ishmael, and Isaac, and Jacob, and the Patriarchs, and in what was given to Moses and Jesus, and in what was given to the prophets from their Lord. We do not differentiate between any of them. And to Him, we surrender.”
(2:136 / Translated by Community)

Salam (Peace) -

author: @analuisaarkesteijntenan2538
text:
Egypt was a dream trip for my family, and I’m very happy that I’ve been to Cairo and the Valley of the Kings once in my life. Seeing the pyramids with my own eyes, standing inside them, and seeing Tutankamon, , his mask, and all those incredible treasures was truly magical. The kids absolutely loved it, and it was unforgettable to witness what that civilization achieved thousands of years ago.
However, I would never go back again. Unfortunately, tourists are constantly treated like walking bags of money. You can barely breathe without being surrounded by people asking for tips, money, or trying to sell something. Even if you already bought a papyrus or another souvenir, they keep coming and often won’t leave you alone unless you give them money. At times, it felt impossible to walk peacefully with our family and guide because there were always another 6–10 people following us everywhere. It became very exhausting and disturbing.

The guides were not much better. We paid a fortune for private guides, expecting detailed explanations inside the museums and historical sites, but instead they rushed us through everything so we would have more time (and money) to spend in shops connected to their friends or family (?). 

The beach hotels were also disappointing in this regard. Even at an all-inclusive resort, the staff constantly expected tips for every little thing — even for serving a glass of water at the swimming pool.

So yes, I would still recommend that everyone visit Egypt at least once because the history and monuments are extraordinary. But do not expect a relaxing or quiet holiday. It will be extremely hot, incredibly impressive, and at the same time, very overwhelming.

author: @raucousmortuary
text: It was disappointing to see so much misinformation about both sides of Niagara Falls. Yes, the Canada side is touristy. But it also has world-class views, many different amazing experiences (Cave of the Winds, Maid of the Mist, The Tunnel) to see the Falls up close, which is exciting as hell no matter how many others are there. Also, southern Ontario has great rural spaces including wineries, scenic gorges, and Niagara-on-the-Lake, a gorgeous region. The US side most certainly does NOT "have only one observation deck". There is one TOWER, but multiple ways to view the falls in a more natural setting than Canada including their own Cave of the Winds and Maid of the Mist. There are also many other attractions in the area" Whirlpool Park, Devil's Hole with its spectacular and challenging hikes, Fort Niagara, Power Vista, and the Aquarium. This video is full of it. Totally worth visiting especially in September when the crowds leave but everything is still open.

text: I remember visiting the historic section of Galveston with my mom on a bus tour of Texas.  We were let out at the visitor's center and informed that they were filming a movie in the historic area.  We could go in anyway, if we stayed out of the cameras' way.  Mom and I said, let's go.  We found them filming exterior shots along one street, and located a hamburger stand behind the camera set up.  We had lunch watching them film antique cars and a tram over and over,  talked to a few extras in costume, and took pictures of the cameras and a wagon full of palm trees.  It was great.  When we got back to the visitor's center, we discovered no one else had left the building, scared off by the idea of the camera crew.  A vacation is what you make of it.

Yes, there are venders who try and scam with excursions, that is why you book with a travel agent, hotel or another reputable vender. 

In terms of the disappointment with places like Piza, I have been there and I found it to be fantastic because I understood what I was going to see in terms of history and there are literally dimensions of the tower available to anyone, lol. Venice was stinky and pricy but it was incredible, again in terms of history and culture. Maybe book a legit cooking class or take in some entertainment. author: @krab1791
text:
Las Ramblas, Barcelona lol in 2007 we were strolling down the sidewalk in the evening (with a 8 year old and a 15 year old) and my husband and I looked up to see a few transvestites. We immediately turned around.

But I found the subway to be more of a pick pocket hazard. I had my 8 year old by the hand as we stepped on. Immediately 2 men stepped between us. I got loud and didnt let go of my son. I also had my money in a pouch under my clothing. The two men immediately got off.

The worst pick pocket experience I had tho was in Rome near the Colosseum. We decided to take a short cut on our way back to our hotel. I was immediately mobbed by a bunch of kids one of whom had a square of cardboard which she pushed up against my chest. The idea is that while she got my attention, one of the other kids would be underneath picking my pocket. Too bad for them my money was in a pouch they didnt have access too.

I’ve been to visit the Pyramids. It was a 4 day stop as a part of our honeymoon. Luckily we had booked through travel agency so we had guides. We werent bothered by people trying to seperate us from our money. The Pyramids are a site to see.

Trevi Fountain is very crowded (thus the high rate of pick pockets). It’s very beautiful.

author: @johncraven9719
text:
The subject seems a non-starter.

One might think that living or realising a dream would always give pleasure to someone. But buying a product without really asking the right questions is almost bound to lead to worries, anger, disappointment.
BTW one of life's principles is "disappointment is the inevitable result of expecting too much". Many would book a cheap hôtel in order to increase their holiday spending budget or because of their youthful enthusiasm, but would be stupid to complain about the lack of comfort or quiet!
Those who travel while constantly comparing things to "how it was back home" are also bound to become narrow-minded and dissatisfied. To voyage with a sense of curiosity and discovery is a better mindset.

Then again do you want sandy beaches, mountain lakes, hiking, total relaxing, a hired car, being part of a tourist guided group, alone, with someone special? Have you got travel within the country sorted out? Do you know what currency is used (e.g. not every member country of the European Union uses the Euro as currency!)

author: @missusd1130
text: To avoid in Wisconsin: My home state: Events: The WI state fair, for much of the reasons stated here, also drinking events in Madison, for CRIME reasons, including being given knockout and rape drugs, esp. during Freak fest and the Mifflin street block party, and The Hodag Festival which turns out to be mostly a hidden Stugris drug, drunk, prostitute and hidden theft fest on hidden steroids.  If you want family friendly, county fairs are the way to go.  If you need something "more exciting", I suggest the Dells preferably out of the most active time--less expsneisve, less crowded, and way easier to schedule in the "off season" between mid August-mid June.  Yeah, avoid July visits.  Milwaukee should be avoided entirely unless you want to see things like the Betty Brin and other museums outside of crowded times.  Stay out of Milwaukee at night!  Don't park except in paid places, You car MIGHT be watched in those areas.  Most other places in WI are great including Door County, but look for a mosquito or tick report.  You'll thank me.  Biting flies can get guaranteed when those are active and some don't give a rip about DEET.  We've had a first hand experience of DEET failing to even affect ticks and biting flies, mine included a cases of Babiosis.  My recommendation, Visit in August through November to take advantage of the lovely foliage and harvest fest which can be quite family friendly and still have the brewskis you're wanting.  Easily Spring in great too, the bugs are still dead and the worst thing you'll experience is mud.  The camping is best in May and early June but in some places the ticks are out already.  August if ideal drying out and the sicks start to die out.  In SD where I live now stay away from Crazy Horse Monument.  It's not done and insanely expensive.  THey're right about Mt Rushmore, the parking is insanely expensive and don't visit on a cloudy day!  Buy a start park pass the SD state parks are all great, not just Custer and really worth visiting and has less ticks and mosquitoes than WI except during July  Outside of July and January/February and with a careful following of weather reports (because in May we get summer one day and winter the next LOL!).  If you're a cheapie, Spearfish anyone is FREE to explore!  And the city of Spearfish is a tourist paradise with not too far Rapids City having a lot of great sights, places to visit and even hiking areas that are great views and not hard to manage.

author: @SannaFinland
text:
When one's government warns against travelling, resonable people have lost all interest in visiting the country a long ago. Who wants to travel to a country that doesn't respect your privacy, has no respect for the rest of the world, believes in guns instead of healthcare and which citizens are flocking to other countries in panic? So much so that proper countries have tighten access of US citizens. 

Yet, so many lovely places in the world so welcomming. Why choose a destination in a hostile country?  

Even the offering in USA is very limited. Much more impressive nature in Canada that comes with sane and welcoming society. Asian touristic offering is, unlike USA, 21st century comfort. And Europe has the offering for more than one lifetime. Offering in many African and South American  countries has bypassed US offering. 

USA was never the dream destination for the majority of global travellers on curent millenium. For most Europeans it used to be the cheap flights alternative. USA has never been the most beautiful destination, rather far from it. USA used to be a popular shopping destination but in a global world same crap can be found online. Disney can be visited in France or Japan more  and combined with good food, proper sites, vibrant night life and safety. There are gambling destinations cross the world. 

Negatives on US travel, besides safety concerns, include too long distances for convenience, not that much to see or do, lack of public transportation, limited activities, low accommondation quality, horrible food quality, scams like tipping, price tags not maching the actual prices, lack of heating indoors, lack of affordable healthcare, hostile and violent culture. The cost to quality ratio is one of the worst in the world. 

At 2026 the attractiveness of USA travel in developed countries is on the same level as visiting Ethiopia, Sudan, Nigeria or Myanmar. Some do it, mainly for family or business reasons, but the majority doesn't even consider it as an option. Nor is it something that is likely to change.

author: @Bminutes
text:
The tipping one will be so hard for me if I ever go. In the US, tipping is very much expected and it's rude not to. I live in Vegas which is a service industry town, so I tip VERY well. I went to the Philippines where they're kind of neutral on tipping. Like, they will be thankful if you do, but it's not expected and I still had the urge to make sure I tip well.

I have heard the tipping thing in Japan is also changing in recent years, that it's rare for anyone to get offended by it, but it's still not expected.

author: @StoryWeavers0102
text:
私は日本という国に深い敬意と関心を持っています。
日本の規律ある社会、時間を大切にする姿勢、そして努力する人が正当に評価される文化に強く惹かれています。
私は現在、バングラデシュに住んでおり、17歳です。
高校10年生として勉強している学生で、学業には真剣に取り組んでいます。
自分の努力と責任感によって、将来をより良いものにしたいと考えています。
残念ながら、私の国では腐敗や詐欺、賄賂といった社会的な問題が多く、
誠実に努力する人が十分に評価されにくい現実があります。
そのため、私は努力・誠実さ・規律が尊重される環境で
自分自身を成長させたいと強く思うようになりました。
私は日本で日本語と文化を学び、
将来的には社会の一員として責任を果たし、
法律を守り、周囲に良い影響を与えられる人間になりたいと考えています。
この目標を達成するために、日本語学習は不可欠です。
もしご指導やサポートをいただける機会があれば、
常に感謝の気持ちを忘れず、誠実に努力することをお約束します。

The *ORDER* in Japan all stems from the Tokugawa Shogunate which locked down Japan for TWO-HUNDRED-SIXTY-FIVE years (1603-1868) meaning that no one entered or exited the country whatsoever, during which period they all worked together and all taught themselves everything from fishing to farming to martial arts and everything in between. 


The *CIVILITY* in Japan all stems from Gen. Douglas MacArthur who, after Japan surrendered and ended WWII, moved into the HOTEL NEW GRAND in Yokohama and who was not an evil tyrant, but who gave Japan its own Constitution and whose men were all picked for their own manners and charm, not to mention for their good looks.^


The *QUALITY* in Japan all stems from an American Statistician named *DR. W. EDWARDS DEMING* who was sent there after WWII by the US Government under a two year contract, to conduct a Census, but after work most evenings he would go to various nightclubs and play the “guitar, piano and drums and also sing and dance!” In the process he made many friends and upon completion of his contract the Government of Japan asked him to stay behind and teach them about quality to which he amassed the top twenty-one Industrialists in the country and explained both how to *MEASURE* quality and how to *CONTROL* quality to which the Government of Japan - through the J.U.S.E. - established the “DEMING PRIZE COMMITTEE” medal so to this day, the Highest Award that anyone in Japan can win for quality is named after an American! 


^As an aside, when he arrived in Japan MacArthur sequestered himself in said “Hotel New Grand” at at his first dinner there with his staff he said, *”Boys, this is the greatest adventure in military history. text: I visited Amsterdam many many years ago and had such a great time. I was amazed at the sites and the people. I do remember being in the red light district and seeing signs specifically telling people not to take pictures. And of course, there’s an American peeking around the corner with his camera taking pictures. So many tourists that behave badly ruin it for those that respect it all. It’s very sad.

author: @pbjsilverstudio4882
text: As the world population continues to grow, this problem will not get better. I have a problem with social media influencers and YouTubers who promote the beautiful and amazing places around the world without also qualifying their information with the realities of what this places are really facing. I moved to another country and we chose a place that was peaceful and beautiful. But it is also a place that is sometimes promoted by YouTubers and our cities is now under so much construction it’s scary. Why? Because it is in a desert and water is a precious commodity. WE are hyper aware of that fact and take GREAT CARE with our water usage. But most foreigners who now live here full or part time don’t even think about it. There are large fountains in some of their homes and lots of swimming pools even though we are surround by or protected water ways and ocean.   Now we see condos going up all over the city and that scares me. I swear it would be better if rich people bough those plots of land and built big fancy homes because it would be just one household using the water, not multiple households who use the water without a thought.

author: @WrldExplorer
text:
We already did our 10 day trip to Spain last week. We will be back to Europe for three weeks come October, and hopefully for the 
holidays for the Christmas markets. I retired four years ago, and the great thing is I don’t have to go when my job says I can go. So now I travel during shoulder seasons. Some locations will still be crowded regardless, but it won’t be summer crowds with added heat and humidity.

author: @ninjabookitty
text: I like to find random festivals and events and travel to those. Like the Cat Festival in Belgium or the Lemon Festivals in the Cinque Terre. If you're religious, or just like the pageantry, every town in Italy has a Saint and Feast Day devoted to them. I have entire itineraries saved just based on that, but as always, more time than money. 😹

author: @WrldExplorer
text: @ninjabookitty it would be nice to find those unplanned local cultural events. We will keep an eye open. This will be my second time to Rome. I threw a 🪙 over my shoulder, that guaranteed a second visit. 😂  My partner, this is her first time. We recently discussed adding Florence in. My second time also. I told her she would love Florence.

author: @vangrails
text: You are very welcome but those overtouristy places are no longer fun to visit. There are plenty of places in Europe not (yet) hit by overtourism. Do some reading, inform yourself. Visit for example Leiden (my city) instead of Amsterdam.

author: @andersgulowsen2814
text:
I am from Norway, and have a questien to the ppl, and fans in Morocco. I have wondered about this since 1998, but never asked.
And you have a damn good team atm. Gz on that.
When Norway beat Brazil in 1998. (Yes I am 51 so I was Downtown in Oslo celbrating.)
You had the Moroccon fans that live in Norway started making trouble in Oslo. It never got out of hand too much, other than some minor scraps.
Probably because it was maybe 200 thousand ppl cheering, and 1000 pissed Moroccons. I am not joking 80-90% of Morocons was edging for a fight.
I can understand you got dissapointed, as you assumed Brazil would win or at least Draw. But that the country you actually live in, by your own choice, Goes forward, I would think that you would be happy for our sake. (For the record this is people that moved to Norway many years earlier, because they wanted to, not refugees etc).. 
This might sound long winded, but. If the tables was turned. How would you treat me, as a Norwegian, if I was living in any of the big citys in Morocco and started to spit (yes that did happen, Moroccon fans was spitting on the Norwgian flag, and sometimes people) at Moroccon flags and people. Because Norway lost due to Morocco beating another team? I suspect that would not go well for me. Tell me if I am wrong, but I doubt Moroccons would show the same restraint.

Me personally if I Moved to say Marrakesh with my family, to start a new life.


I would think. Well.. shit.. but at least Norway lost to my second favorate team. And go out in the streets to celebrate.

Are our cultures that different? 
Dont get me wrong, Moroccons are great. A summer some years back I was talking to a man From Morocco, just in the neighborhood, on a bench.
I asked, Is Tajin your national dish? Because I heard about it, and it is supposed to be insanly tasty, I can not remember he's name. But or the reply was this. Everyone it that little park between buidings was invited in 2 days (it ws 15 ppl ish) my family wil make you all Tajiin. You are all welcome.

No Norwegian would ever do that.. So maybe difference is a good thing, as long as we are willing to learn from each other.
I learned a lesson. 1 man can make a huge differnce, and change opinions that some have of another Nation to the better.

Ohh the food was AMAZING.. Happy New year and GL in the WC

author: @tomwright4450
text:
Morocco’s World Cup Victory — More Than Just a Game

Morocco winning the World Cup would be far more than just a sporting achievement — it would mark a profound shift in mindset, confidence, and national identity.

I’m 67 years old, and I still remember the legendary 1972 World Chess Championship between Bobby Fischer and Boris Spassky. Many saw it as simply a chess match, but it was so much more — it symbolized a clash between two global superpowers during the Cold War. When Fischer won, it reshaped how Americans viewed themselves: capable of mastering intellect, precision, and strategy on the world stage.

Similarly, Morocco’s rise represents more than football. For decades, France held a strong cultural and economic influence over North and West Africa — from language to commerce. But today, Morocco designs and manufactures its own cars, builds its own supply chains, and stands proudly as an economy as modern and capable as many in Europe.

By winning the World Cup — with Moroccan players and a Moroccan coach — the nation would send a powerful message: we can compete, lead, and win on our own terms.

This victory would inspire generations, boost national pride, and reinforce Morocco’s growing global presence — economically, culturally, and psychologically.

Having lived in Morocco, I can say this: it’s a remarkable country, full of warmth, creativity, and resilience. Its people, food, and culture are second to none. And now, the world is starting to see what Morocco has always known about itself.

author: @atlasmountains
text:
Coach Pereira, it is vital that the players play for the badge, not for themselves. This is a national team with 40 million Moroccans watching from every corner of the globe. An assist, a goal-line save, or a hard-won tackle shows the unselfishness we crave. Above all, play with the heart of a Moroccan Lion. If you play for the team, we will chant your name louder than for any fancy dribble or individual goal.

Treinador Pereira, é vital que os jogadores joguem pelo escudo, e não por si próprios. Esta é uma seleção nacional com 40 milhões de marroquinos a assistir em todos os cantos do mundo. Uma assistência, um corte em cima da linha ou uma dividida ganha demonstram o altruísmo que tanto desejamos. Acima de tudo, joguem com o coração de um Leão de Atlas. Se jogarem pela equipa, gritaremos o vosso nome com mais força do que por qualquer drible fútil ou golo individual.

author: @TamenfoustAli
text:
Pour l'histoire,
- Le défunt roi Hassan 2, avait signé avec l’Algérie, le 15 juin 1972, à l’occasion du sommet de l’OUA tenu à Rabat, une convention sur le tracé de la frontière d’État entre les deux pays. Ce texte souligne que ces «dispositions règlent définitivement les questions de frontière entre l’Algérie et le Maroc ». 
   - Le makhzen n'arrête pas de raconter des mensonges. L’Algérie PROPRIETAIRE de Gara Djebilet situé dans son territoire. et le Maroc disposant de la possibilité d’évacuation et d’embarquement du minerai sur l’Atlantique, ont établi une convention de création d’une société mixte 50/50 ( SAM) en 1972. pour la COMMERCIALISATION du minerai. Le Maroc devait construire la voie ferrée et le port sur l'Atlantique. A ce jour, le Maroc n'a rien fait. Et comme l'Algérie, après plusieurs années d’attente, a décidé de construire sa propre voie ferrée, le Maroc proteste, et invente des mensonges. 
   - Le défunt roi est le 1er à ne pas croire que le Sahara Occidental est marocain. Il avait signé un arrêté paru au bulletin officiel du royaume N° 3311 du 16/04/1976 officialisant le partage du Sahara occidental avec la Maurétanie. - il avait accepté l'organisation du référendum par l'ONU en 1980, et avait accepté un cesser le feu en 1991 avec le Polisario. 
   - A la mort de Hassan, le makhzen se prosterne devant le nouveau roi ( fils de Hassan 2 ) et met à son service pour renier toutes les actions d’état du père

text:
La gambie 23 fois plus petite que le Sahara occidental ne reconnait pas la RASD 🇪🇭💪😂 mais reconnait le plan d autonomie sur le sahara occidental qui represente l'equivalent de 60% du territoire marocain😂. La question est : quel pays au monde dans toute l' histoire de l'humanité a revendiqué un territoire equivalent à 60% de la superficie de son propre territoire ?
Réponse : aucun sauf le Merdoc 💩😅 qui pue la félonie et la malhonnêteté et il le sait pertinemment que le sahara occidental 🇪🇭💪n'a jamais été marocain.Preuve est que c'est le roi hassan 2 lui- même  qui a inscrit le sahara occidental 🇪🇭💪en 1963 à l'ONU sous la résolution 1514 des territoires non autonomes à décoloniser et non comme territoire revendiqué par le Merdoc. La mauvaise foi et les mensonges ont toujours étè le fond de commerce des charlatans narcotraficants alaouite. A bon entendeur.

text: Que le Maroc commence par le commencement en demandant aux sahraouis qui sont les principaux concernés de reconnaître la marocanité de leur territoire et le problème est réglé  Où est l'opportunité de ces reconnaissances par des pays qui n'ont rien à voir avec cette question dès lors qu'elles sont  totalement inutiles et inopérantes,, puisque le dossier est inscrit comme étant à décoloniser par l'ONU, et que la solution est en route dans le cadre des négociations en cours entre le Polisario et le Maroc sous l'égide de Massaad Boulos et Stéphane De Mistura au nom de l'ONU. On a l'impression que le Maroc pratique la politique de l'autruche sachant que seules les discussions en cours ont un sens, et une chance d'aboutir, et non pas cette course aux reconnaissances qui ne  sont d 'aucun poids même si elles entretiennent l'illusion d'une diplomatie qui active, et, qui ferait avancer les choses.

author: @ILikeAppol
text:
Lake Chargoggagoggmanchauggagoggchaubunagungamaugg is a 45-letter, 14-syllable Nipmuc phrase for a Massachusetts lake, often poetically translated as "You fish on your side, I fish on my side, nobody fish in the middle." Located in Webster, this landmark boasts more 'g's than average feet of depth, serving as a famous tourist attraction. [1, 2, 3, 4] 
------------------------------
## The Speech: Pride of the 15 'G's
Friends, tourists, and residents of this fine town of Webster, Massachusetts, we gather today by the shores of the most illustrious body of water in the United States. I speak, of course, of Lake Chargoggagoggmanchauggagoggchaubunagungamaugg! [2, 5] 
Some call it Webster Lake. Some, perhaps lacking the linguistic stamina, call it "the lake." But for those with a flair for the historic and the extraordinary, we know it by its full, glorious, 45-letter name. [2, 6, 7] 
Imagine, if you will, the early days of this great land. The Nipmuc people, in their wisdom, knew this place as Chaubunagungamaugg, simply translating to "fishing place at the boundary". But as time flowed on, and the English settlers arrived, the boundary became a meeting place—a neutral ground for two cultures to share in the bounty of these waters. [2, 7] 
It was here, over 100 years ago, that the name was coined (or perhaps expanded) to reflect this unity, sometimes jokingly cited as: [2, 3, 7] 

"You fish on your side, I fish on my side, nobody fish in the middle."

It is a name that defines us. It is a name that mocks the timid, short-word-loving world. With fifteen ‘g’s—yes, fifteen—it stands as a testament to linguistic endurance! [2, 4, 8] 
There are many lakes in the world, my friends. There are lakes with short names, lakes with simple names, and lakes with boring names. But there is only one lake that makes you stutter, only one that challenges your spelling bee skills, and only one that serves as a beacon of pride for our town. [2, 4, 7, 8] 
Poet Bertha A. Joslin told us in the 1950s:
"Touch not a g! No impious hand shall wrest one from that noble name. Fifteen in all their glory stand. And ever shall the same." [9] 
So, whether you are traversing the waters of North, Middle, or South Pond, or just trying to read the sign on the I-395 overpass, take pride in the mouthful.
Behold the mighty, the long, the unforgettable—Chargoggagoggmanchauggagoggchaubunagungamaugg! [2, 6, 7, 10] 
------------------------------
## Quick Facts about the Name

* Location: Webster, Massachusetts.
* Significance: Longest place name in the U.S. (45 letters, 15 'g's).
* Meaning: Often interpreted as "You fish on your side, I fish on my side, nobody fish in the middle," but more accurately means "Fishing place at the boundaries—Neutral Meeting Grounds".
* Alternative Names: Webster Lake, Lake Chaubunagungamaug.
* Pronunciation Key: Char-gog-a-gog-man-chog-a-gog-cha-bun-a-gung-a-maug.

author: @user-oc8jp2bk2y
text:
I am not into this music genre, but why does this art feels like the essence of my childhood?

Huh, that was pretty intense
I barely listened to music, I just paused, started at the art, felt something and wrote that comment, and now I am editing it telling that apprently it helped me
This does convey the vibe of my childhood, and I had to reprocess it for a bit
My childhood mostly was not happy, and mostly, traumatic shit happened to happen at summers, so I spent most either gaming or walking with friends outside (most of them were not doing okay too)
So... I recalled some memories and had to cry for the first time about it, I would probably get to that later otherwise but it happened here

author: @stevereviews2026
text:
@brittanica1 you need to read "Don't let's go to the dogs tonight" by Alexandra Fuller, a story of growing up on a farm in Rhodesia. It's hilarious and eye-opening in equal measure. I left SA in 2011 and you don't realise the constant stress you're living under until you leave. I still have my head on a swivel.
In SA I've been burgled about 8 times, had two cars stolen, been mugged 7 times, my aunt and uncle were held up at gunpoint in their home, my dad was stabbed in the chest for his payroll and that's more or less standard for all South Africans who have similar stats.
South Africa was paradise, in fact, Capetonians never went on holiday, we were on holiday.
You dodged a bullet by avoiding Hanover Park, it could have been much worse, they could have taken you to Valhalla Park, Lavender Hill or Manenberg, Khayelitsha, Crossroads or Gugulethu.
I was in the SADF when the IFP and ANC were killing each other in a turf war in Natal. We pulled out truckloads of guns and improvised weapons.
More vicious and violent people you won't meet.

author: @schweinhund7966
text: After spending 6 months in a tent in Central America with the US Army, I took a 35 day vacation to South Africa; Zimbabwe and Botswana in 1985.  I drove solo from Johannesburg, Pretoria, Phalaborwa; Graskop; Pilgrim’s Rest; kruger National Park; Carolina; Royal Natal National Park; Rorke’s Drift; Islandlwana; And Durban; flew to Port Elizabeth and drove to Oudtshoorn, Cape Agulhas and Cape Town for a total of 4K kilometers (manual transmission and NO GPS) over 28 days.  It was an incredible safe, comfortable and eye-opening “adventure.”  I flew on to Zimbabwe and Botswana before heading to Asia for my next assignment.  I returned to South Africa in 2015 and could see the obvious deterioration!  Apartheid was stupid but what has replaced it is blatant corruption and serious decline.  I can only imagine what Rhodesia was like.

author: @Robespierre-II
text:
The problem with this is that the extent to which South Africa was a developed country previously is questionable.  

It had a capitalist economy based on resource extraction - the famous example was diamond extraction.  That wealth was shared amongst the ruling class in apartheid and used to keep that political system going for a few decades.  When it collapsed, everyone who was kept in poverty all that time had many reasons to force through both pleasant and unpleasant changes.   

I'm afraid this is a "your grandparents caused this" situation.

author: @Centrist1985
text:
British Visitor Here. 

I have been to the Philippines twice - in 2024 and 2026

The only other Asian country i've been to is China in 2013 and 2018-19

I think it is a pity the Philippine tourism sector lags behind other Asian countries because the Philippines genuinely has a lot to offer

In my experience, the biggest problem is poor organization.

Back in the UK now. I visited Cagbalete Island a few weeks ago with my girlfriend's family. The island has genuinely great sand, snorkeling opportunities, clear water and an impressive fire-dance show- there are a lot of ingredients for it being an ideal beach resort

But when we arrived we had to wait 3 hours before the lodge was ready - not very comfortable when you just want to get settled in- instead of taking responsibility, the guy that was supposed to manage our tour put his mother in charge!  

The food there was also lacklustre. My sister-in-law made a formal complaint back on the mainland but this is typical of shoddy management

On my first trip, we went to Mt Pinatubo only to be told that as a foreigner I couldn't enter because of a military base there. We were compensated by the company but it was a wasted day - they failed to do their research and inform us before hand. 

I don't say these things to be overtly negative. I've also had good experiences in the Philippines - my visits to the Villa Escudero Colonial Mansion estate, Intramuros in Manila and Hesus Na Kamay statue in Tiaong, the Corregidor Island excursion  among other sites were positive. 


But we also visited Ancestral Homes that only offered guided tours to groups - why lose potential customers by not offering couples too? 

I still have an overall positive feel about the Philippines - maybe I am biased, but I do think some common sense improvements can be made - better organization, more flexibility. 

In summary - 

Positive -  Underappreciated sites and a unique culture, the best buffet restaurants i've ever been to, room for lots of potential

Negative - More often than not, poor organization, lack of flexibility in appealing to tourists

To be fair, my experience probably wasn't too bad because I was always in the company of my Filipina partner and her family - I can imagine travelling alone, i'd probably be scammed as I was in China

I still love the Philippines and that is why i'd like to see the tourism sector grow

author: @Asmarman3
text:
L'objectif n'est pas de se qualifier pour la Coupe du monde, mais de concourir pour le titre et de battre les grandes équipes. Les équipes arabes et africains sont loin de cela, à l'exception du Maroc, toujours premier dans tous les disciplines notamment de l' athlétisme du Aouita, et motawakil en 84 passant par le hicham el garouje salah hissou, bakali...la liste est longue.
En foot, le premier point pour les Arabes et l' Afrique dès leur première participation en 1970. En 1986, ils passent le premier tour à se qualifier en tant que leader du  groupe relevé. Enfin, ils sont les premiers à se qualifier pour les demi-finales de la Coupe du monde au Qatar en 2022. 
Aucune équipe arabe et africaine qui ne leur ressemble aujourd'hui, surtout depuis 2018. Le Maroc est le visage du football africain et arabe, non seulement parmi les grandes équipes, mais dans toutes les catégories : 
champion d'Afrique U17 à deux reprises et demi-finaliste de la dernière Coupe du monde en 2023. 
Champion d'Afrique U20, est désormais champion du monde en 2025 au Chili.  
champion d'Afrique U23, et médaillés de bronze olympiques en 2024 à Paris, 
l'équipe locale, triple championne d'Afrique d' affilié (2018, 2020 et 2024). 
Et enfin, l'équipe senior, quatrième au monde en demi-finale en 2022.
 Sans oublier les équipes féminines et de futsal, où le Maroc domine l'Afrique et le monde arabe avec sept coupes consécutives… Le Maroc est le seul pays à avoir d'avantages des talons dans tous les championnats continentaux et mondiaux…

author: @manuhari3747
text: Le Maroc ne se résume pas à son couscous sa cuisine ses paysages son soleil et ses plages . Il est plutôt un pays riche d'une longue histoire de gloires et d'héroïsme . Il est plus ancien et plus vénérable que la France . À l'époque où il possédait la plus vieille université du monde  les rues de France étaient noyées sous la boue . author: @johnhopkins4628
text:
During my tour on Essex in the 90's; if our CO was an Aviator, then the XO would be a SWO.  And if the CO was a SWO, then the XO would be an Aviator.  Ironically, the Aviation XO's and CO I had on Essex were from the P-3 Community and not from the Rotor Wing Community.   I had one XO on my 96-97 Cruise, Andy Winns, who fleeted up to be our CO, and his XO, was a seasoned SWO, and CAPT Winns relied upon him heavily as he had never been underway on a  warship until he had been assigned to Essex.

 I do recall that Lincoln had a H-3/SH-60 Pilot as CO while she was in RCOH, CAPT Ron Ravelo.  I'd deployed with Ron aboard Ranger when he was in HS-14 (and had worked with his Dad at SPAWAR).  

 However, the question at hand, is the Material Condition and readiness of our warships, and that goes beyond whether the CO is a SWO or an Aviator.  If you don't have trained and competent professionals to sail and maintain our vessels, they are going to fail; if you don't have an adequate COSAL or AVCAL, you don't have adequate spare parts, tools, or test equipment readily available, you're going to fail.  Technicians can't repair a ship with just knowledge and air.  I have friends, retired Chiefs and LDO's who work at MARMC, who are doing work on ships that they used to perform when they were active.  The senior enlisted, the folks who are supposed to be subject matter experts, well in too many cases, they can't tell a bulkhead from an overhead, and give a puzzled look when you say 'deckplate' but they can navigate the various HR, feel good programs that have nothing to do with being a Warrior.  But in the meantime we put a band aid on things and find a scapegoat to hide the failings of the organization instead of taking a critical look in the mirror.

author: @sec808
text:
Towards the end of your discussion, you kind of hit on exactly what the CNO stated about the readiness of the Amphib fleet. Currently to the aviation community the Amphibs are thought to a training tour, so they are ready to command "the important ship". Amphib community often has to fight for money similar to the Marine Corps. These ships need COs who have spent their entire careers polishing their knowledge and skills to maintain and operate them. I was surprised that LHAs and LHDs aren't in the list that aviators can command, submarine tender command will definitely train the COs on the maintenance required by ships, but they usually do VERY little steaming.

When I was Radar Nav/ASUW Officer on USS Nimitz the ANAV asked me why I didn't have his job because I was a Navigator on a cruiser during my last tour and he had never navigated or 'drove' a ship in his life. I was an O-3 and the senior person (outside of the Engineering Plant) involved in the operation of the ship. The Command Investigation of the Truman collision mentions this directly. Perhaps the best path is for the Navy to really step back and evaluate why the aviation community needs to be who commands an aircraft carrier. This requirement started way before the CAG became a major command, there may lots of utility to improve the CAG/CO relationship which could diminish the benefits of have an aviator vice SWO as carrier CO. Let the people who have been 'raised from birth' to command and drive the ships, and the aviators ensure the ship properly supports the mission.

I'm expecting a lot of angry aviators replying to this, but there is a constant push to specialize the SWOs into deck or engineering like the Merchant fleet, all while the Navy requires a senior aviator to suddenly be able to command the Navy's largest and most complex moving city on the planet after having two years of a driver education course.

author: @kblackav8or
text: I was the N8 then the N5A then N5 at a ESG/CPG towards the end of my career.  There for the transition from CPG being converted to ESG's and the "Classron" debacle.  I can kind of understand where they are coming from but also the merit of having Aviators command LHA/LHD ships for at least part of the time.  The Command alternated between SWO and Aviator Admirals and under the ESG model USMC generals.  The SWO world doesn't have good major command options anymore.  The CG's are almost gone and reverted down to O-5 commands largely so what's left for the O-6's?  DESRON and PHIBRON etc but not actual ship commands in any decent numbers.  We don't have a proper surface combatant ship these days with the facilities to house a Admirals staff that isn't a Aviation ship and I don't really count the welded to the pier LCC's.  Did the LCC's go to the Gulf for the current events.  Nope.  We need a proper cruiser sized cruiser fleet once again based on a hull with enough space to house a strike group staff that isn't a CVN.  Yea very likely we will generally always have a CVN around for that but not always.  Burkes are great but they are pretty much run their course for space and design on what we can cram into that hull and powertrain.  We have the silly Zumwalts which has been a unmitigated disaster.. The Constellations.. another dorked up mess..Little Crappy ship.. and the Ford's have had their teething issues to be sure.  The LPD's were nearly a disaster, I was on staff for the first one to show up in the gulf and it spent much of it's deployment having major repairs for what was really contractor/builder malpractice and a major gaff in quality control.  We are lucky we didn't lose one in a major fire.  It was bad.  So what is the up and coming SWO O-6 left to command.  LPD's are O-6 commands and in the past the LHA/LHD's would alternate between Aviators and SWO's.  My post jumps around but the root point is there hasn't been a good end command ship path for the SWO's for a while.  CG/CGN was it for decades post BB era.  The gator navy just doesn't have the same respect and you still need a platform for a perspective CVN Skipper to hone his ship handling and ship running skillset outside of the disassociated stuff they may get on CVN's when younger.  It's kind of an arbitrary decision.  I don't entirely oppose it but when MSC runs all the tankers these days, that path is gone.  Used to be pretty common for up and coming CV/CVN Skippers to do that tour.  Side note.  I rode CNO's sub twice for a couple weeks at a time during Connie BG work ups and deployment in 94/95.  He's a solid dude.  Great choice for CNO.  One addition on edit.  LHA/LHD was often a Helo Aviator capstone command for quite some time.  That takes away that and there are a lot of us and some who are definitely solid folks more than capable of exceling at it.

author: @YahyaLachgar-v4c6t
text:
نعم، انتشرت في الأيام الأخيرة قصة شاب سويسري من أصل مغربي يُدعى يوناس لاوفينر، وأثارت ضجة كبيرة في الإعلام ومواقع التواصل.
القصة تتعلق بأنه أعلن نفسه بشكل رمزي “ملكًا” أو “إمبراطورًا” في Switzerland، واستغل ثغرات قانونية مرتبطة بالأراضي غير المسجلة ليستحوذ على عدد كبير من الأراضي والعقارات. �
صحيفة الخليج +2
وبحسب التقارير:
بدأ الأمر منذ سنوات عبر مراسم “تتويج” رمزية في مدينة Bern.
أنشأ ما يشبه “إمبراطورية” خاصة به وموقعًا إلكترونيًا.
تمكن من تسجيل عشرات بل مئات القطع الأرضية باسمه مستفيدًا من ثغرة في القانون المدني السويسري. �
فوشيا +1
وبعض المصادر ذكرت أنه امتلك حوالي 149 إلى 151 قطعة أرض موزعة على عدة كانتونات سويسرية، بينها غابات وأجزاء من طرق. �
الجزيرة نت +1
لكن مهم توضيح شيء:
هو ليس ملكًا حقيقيًا لسويسرا 👑
ولا توجد ملكية في سويسرا أصلًا
اللقب رمزي واستعراضي أكثر من كونه منصبًا رسميًا.

author: @BodraMohamed
text:
سلام عليكم.
هده الصقطة غير مبرر من أستاد وخبير القتصادي وسياسي؟!
المملكة المغربية تبني قدراتها الذاتية مع شركائها التقلديين والجدد ،رابح رابح.
القدرات الصناعية العسكرية المغربية مزالت في بداياتها ،هناك طموح مغربي جارف لكتساب الخبرات الدفاعية والمدنية ورقمنة في أفق استقلالية الذاتية المغربية❤
أما عن الهدف من كل هده الترسانة القانونية وتكوين وانشاء ووضع منصات عالية وكفأت والخبرة اليد والعقول المغربية ليس لمهاجمت أحد بل المثال يقول///اشري السلم بقوة الدبلوماسية وعسكرية وصناعية والاقتصادية والتجارية//
بل المملكة تكون الاطر العسكرية عالية الكفأت المغربية والافريقية لتكون مستقبلا جيش مع حليفه الرئيسي الولايات المتحدة الأمريكية،بقيادة مشتركة تحت قبة أفريكوم انطلاقا من الصحراء الغربية المغربية ❤

text:
المغاربة حكومة و شعبا وقفوا ضد بشار المجرم منذ اول رصاصة أطلقت على صدر الشعب السوري الشقيق و هو يطالب بالحرية التي نالها بإذن الله تعالى و توفيقه
في الجانب الآخر نظام العسكر الجزائري دعم نظام بشار المجرم حتى آخر يوم و يوجد مئات الجزائريين و مرتزقة البوليساريو الكبابية  في سجون سوريا سقطوا اسرى إثر سقوط النظام في نفس الوقت قاتل المغاربة مع المعارضة السورية التي فتح لها الله بالنصر 
قلوبنا معكم يا أهل سوريا 
اللهم وفق السوريين لي بناء وطن آمن مستقر مزدهر و قوي
مغربي مر من هنا

author: @miloudgara3398
text:
المغرب وسوريا دائما كنا اوفياء حتى في ضل الخيانة للمقبور حافظ وابنه المجرم بشار،لاكننا قطعناها مندو بداية التورة على الجرائم عكس من شارك فيها وسكت عليها أما لارضاء إيران او الخوف منها.
مرحبا وألف مرحبا برجال سوريا الاحرار ونسائها الحراتبعدى التورة المجيدة التي سنخلدها معكم بأدن الله بسقوط الطاغية المجرم،حفظ الله سوريا والعراق واليمن ولبنان من المد الشيعي مرة اخرى،الله ينصركم على عدوكم ويتبتكم على عدل الله بين عباده.

author: @salaherrait1485
text:
## تارودانت ومراكش: حكاية "الأصل" الذي طواه النسيان و"المجد" الذي سرق الأضواء
حين نقف اليوم أمام أسوار **تارودانت**، فنحن لا ننظر مجرد سور طيني متهالك، بل نحن أمام "الأم" الشرعية لمدينة مراكش. لكن المفارقة التاريخية المؤلمة هي أن هذه الأم بقيت في الظل، بينما كبرت الابنة (مراكش) لتصبح واجهة العالم، تاركةً خلفها عراقةً تستغيث تحت وطأة الإهمال والزحف الإسمنتي البارد.
### 1. الحقيقة الغائبة: تارودانت هي "المسودة" الأولى لمراكش
يجب أن يدرك الجميع أن تارودانت سبقت مراكش بقرون. كانت هي الحاضرة، وهي المختبر الذي تعلم فيه المرابطون كيف يبنون القلاع ويحصنون المدن قبل أن يضعوا حجر أساس مراكش عام 1062م. إن ما نراه في مراكش من أسوار ونمط حياة، ليس إلا نسخة مطورة وموسعة لما بدأ هنا في سوس. لكن التاريخ لا ينصف دائماً الأقدم، بل ينصف الأقوى سياسياً.
### 2. التهميش الممنهج: كيف سُرقت الروح من سوس؟
لقد تعرضت تارودانت لعملية "استنزاف" تاريخي من قِبل السلالات الحاكمة. السعديون، الذين انطلقوا من قلب سوس وبنوا مجدهم من سكر وفلاحة تارودانت، سرعان ما أداروا ظهرهم لها بمجرد أن استتب لهم الأمر، ونقلوا الثروات والحرفيين والعلماء إلى مراكش. لقد حُكم على تارودانت أن تبقى "مخزناً" للثروة، بينما حُكم لمراكش أن تكون "واجهة" للمجد، وهذا هو التفسير الحقيقي للإهمال الذي نراه اليوم.
### 3. لغز "اللسان الروداني": هويةٌ خلف الأسوار
كثيراً ما يُطرح السؤال: لماذا يتحدث أهل تارودانت بدارجة متميزة وسط محيط سوسي أمازيغي؟ الحقيقة أن هذه اللهجة هي "لغة القلعة". لقد كانت تارودانت مدينة إدارية وتجارية (مخزنية) بامتياز، مما جعلها تتبنى الدارجة كأداة للتواصل مع السلطة ومع القوافل التجارية القادمة من البعيد. هذه اللهجة لم تكن انفصالاً عن الهوية السوسية، بل كانت درعاً ثقافياً ميز "أهل الحضر" داخل الأسوار عن محيطهم.
### 4. المأزق العمراني: حين يُحبس الإسمنت في إطار من طين
ما نراه اليوم في تارودانت يدعو للأسى؛ أسوار تاريخية تتآكل، وبنايات حديثة عشوائية تنبت بداخلها بلا هوية ولا روح. لقد غاب الاستثمار الذي حظيت به مدن كفاس ومراكش، وتُركت تارودانت لمصيرها، مما حولها من "جوهرة أثرية" إلى مجرد تجمع سكاني مشوه بصرياً. الإهمال لم يطال الحجر فقط، بل طال "الجمالية" المغربية التي اندثرت تحت وطأة الحاجة وغياب الرؤية الواضحة للحفاظ على التراث.
### ختاماً:
إن الحديث عن تارودانت ليس بكاءً على الأطلال، بل هو محاولة لاستعادة الاعتبار لمدينة أعطت للمغرب هويته وعمارته وعواصمه، ثم وُضعت على الرف. إن ما تراه عيوننا اليوم من تهالك هو نتيجة لسنوات من تقديم "الواجهات السياحية" على "الجذور التاريخية". تارودانت هي الأصل، ومراكش هي الظل الذي كبر حتى غطى على صاحبه.

text:
مراكش هي تسمية امازيغية سماها المرابطين كعاصمة لهم بما انهم مسلمين سماو عاصمتهم  ارض الله بالخلفية الدينية 
مور = بالأمازيغية الارض 
اكوش = بالأمازيغية الله الخالق 
وتجمع مراكش او اموراكش بالأمازيغية 
وتوجد قرية صغيرة في شرق المغرب بين جبال الاطلس تسما تمراكشت تصغير وتدليل لتلك القرية تيمنا بمراكش المدينة الكبير مراكش
وللمعلومة مور هي نفسها موروكو اي ارض شعب المور بهدا نحن نقول اننا لسنا عرب نحن شعب المور وسكان موروكو هم موريون امازيغ
Morokko
Morocco
Maroc
Marueccos 
جميع شعوب الارض عبر عصور يعلمون ان سكان هده الارض  ومعروف لديهم ان سكان موروكو هم المور .
القوم والوحيدين اللدين  حروفو تسميته مور هما العرب من الخليج ومعروف عن العرب يغيرون تسمية اي قوم لقتل تاريخهم وطمسه مثل تينيس صارت تونس المعنى لهاد بل تينيس هي المفتاح اي مفتاح افريقيا .
اخر انجازات العرب تسمية منتخب جزر الكومر الى جزر القمر 🌙 وصراحة لاعلاقة  😂😂😂😂😂😂 العرب محترفين في تزوير وجب عدم الاخد من مصادرهم في التاريخ
ونزيدكم ترجمة 
المملكة المغربيةادا ترجمت  بالانجليزية هي westernocco 
وليس Morocco 
اما ادا قمنا لترجمة Morocco الى العربية فسنحصل على 
مملكة المور اي شعب المور 
بهده تسمية مغرب هو اتجاه وليس اسم بلد وشعبه لان مملكة المور تنفي العروبة عنها ادا لاتصلح لهم بما انهم يريدون تعريبها 😂😂😂😂😂😂😂😂 الحمد لله على نعمة البصيرة
